# Edict

> A programming language designed exclusively for AI agents. No parser, no syntax — agents produce AST directly as JSON. Statically typed, effect-tracked, contract-verified via Z3, compiled to WebAssembly. Full compiler pipeline exposed via Model Context Protocol (MCP).

## Overview

- [README](https://github.com/Sowiedu/Edict/blob/main/README.md): Project overview, quick start, architecture diagram
- [Feature Specification](https://github.com/Sowiedu/Edict/blob/main/FEATURE_SPEC.md): Complete language specification — types, effects, contracts, modules, execution model
- [Roadmap](https://github.com/Sowiedu/Edict/blob/main/ROADMAP.md): Development plan and priorities
- [Contributing](https://github.com/Sowiedu/Edict/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md): Setup instructions, coding standards, PR workflow

## Using Edict (for AI agents)

Edict is designed for agents, not humans. The canonical workflow:

1. Read the AST JSON Schema to learn the program format
2. Write a program as a JSON AST conforming to the schema
3. Submit to the compiler — if errors, read structured error JSON, fix, resubmit
4. Compile to WASM and execute in sandboxed runtime

Install and run the MCP server:
```
npm install edict-lang
npx edict-lang         # stdio transport
```

## MCP Tools

- `edict_schema`: Returns the full AST JSON Schema (supports full/minimal/compact formats)
- `edict_version`: Returns compiler version and capability info
- `edict_examples`: Returns 18 example programs as JSON ASTs
- `edict_validate`: Validates AST structure (fields, types, node kinds)
- `edict_check`: Full pipeline — validate → resolve → type check → effect check → verify contracts
- `edict_compile`: Compiles checked AST to WASM (returns base64)
- `edict_run`: Executes WASM in sandboxed runtime with configurable limits
- `edict_patch`: Applies targeted AST patches by nodeId and re-checks
- `edict_errors`: Machine-readable catalog of all structured error types
- `edict_lint`: Non-blocking quality warnings (unused vars, missing contracts, etc.)

## MCP Resources

- `edict://schema`: Full AST JSON Schema
- `edict://schema/minimal`: Token-optimized schema (descriptions stripped)
- `edict://examples`: All example programs
- `edict://errors`: Machine-readable error catalog
- `edict://schema/patch`: Patch protocol schema

## Key Resources

- [AST JSON Schema](https://github.com/Sowiedu/Edict/blob/main/schema/edict.schema.json): The formal schema for writing Edict programs
- [Examples](https://github.com/Sowiedu/Edict/tree/main/examples): 18 example programs as JSON ASTs
- [Agent Guide](https://github.com/Sowiedu/Edict/blob/main/AGENTS.md): Agent-facing entry point with quick orientation

## Language Features

- **Type system**: Int, Int64, Float, String, Bool, Array<T>, Option<T>, Result<T,E>, records, enums, refinement types, function types, semantic unit types
- **Effect system**: pure, reads, writes, io, fails — compiler-enforced consistency
- **Contract verification**: Pre/postconditions verified at compile time by Z3/SMT with concrete counterexamples
- **WASM compilation**: Compiles to WebAssembly via binaryen, runs in sandboxed Node.js runtime
- **Security model**: No ambient authority — filesystem, network, crypto access only through explicit host adapters

## Architecture

```
src/
├── ast/           # AST node interfaces
├── validator/     # Schema validation
├── resolver/      # Name resolution (Levenshtein suggestions)
├── checker/       # Type checking (bidirectional)
├── effects/       # Effect checking (call-graph propagation)
├── contracts/     # Contract verification (Z3/SMT)
├── codegen/       # WASM generation (binaryen) + execution
├── builtins/      # Builtin registry
├── compact/       # Token-efficient compact AST format
├── lint/          # Non-blocking quality warnings
├── patch/         # Surgical AST patching by nodeId
├── mcp/           # MCP server (tools + resources + prompts)
└── errors/        # Structured error types
```

## npm Package

- Package: [edict-lang](https://www.npmjs.com/package/edict-lang)
- License: MIT
- Node.js: ≥20.0.0
